Military Mar 29, 2026

USAF E-3 Sentry AWACS damaged in Iranian strike on Saudi air base

An Iranian missile and drone attack on Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia on March 27, 2026,…...

The vulnerability of strategic early-warning aircraft to modern Iranian air defenses demonstrates critical gaps in base protection protocols for allied air operations in the Middle East.

See AeroTime Hub →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Just 2 Hours: The World's Shortest Airbus A380 Route Gets Even More Flights

Two different carriers fly their superjumbos on this corridor.

Ultra-short A380 operations demonstrate viable niche markets for superjumbos beyond long-haul routes, potentially reshaping aircraft deployment strategies across carriers.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

7 Longest Nonstop Flights In The World In 2026

Four out of the seven use the Boeing 787.

Boeing 787's superior fuel efficiency and range capabilities are driving its dominance on ultra-long-haul routes, with the aircraft comprising 57% of the world's longest nonstop flights.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 29, 2026

‘Shots on goal and win the game’: NASA’s effort to accelerate lunar landings

While NASA outlined plans to increase the cadence of robotic lunar lander missions at a recent event, it said little about accelerating work on crewed lunar landers.

NASA's focus on accelerating robotic lunar missions masks delays in crewed lander development, creating a two-track timeline that may constrain human exploration schedules.

See SpaceNews →

Safety Mar 29, 2026

"The Runway Wasn't Clear": Southwest 737 Touches Down Before Abrupt Burbank Go-Around

Reportedly, the pilot announced to the passengers that the runway was not clear when the aircraft touched down, resulting in a go-around.

Operational discipline during approach prevented a potential runway incursion when the crew executed a go-around after detecting an obstruction post-touchdown.

See Simple Flying →

PAI
Safety Mar 29, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS shifts accountability from post-incident investigations to continuous risk identification, requiring operators to embed safety culture throughout their organizations rather than treating compliance as a reactive checkbox.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Military Mar 29, 2026

USAF A-10 strike on Iraqi military base kills seven soldiers

An airstrike attributed to a US Air Force A-10 Thunderbolt II hit a military clinic at the Habbaniyah…...

Close air support precision failures in combat operations highlight the persistent challenge of distinguishing military targets from civilian infrastructure in densely populated conflict zones.

See AeroTime Hub →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Do Airlines Make Money From Economy Seats?

This heavily depends on the carrier and its operations.

Economy seat profitability varies dramatically by airline based on cost structure, load factors, and route networks rather than being a universal revenue driver.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Mar 29, 2026

Alaska Airlines Pilots Ask For Police “To Meet The Airplane” After Passenger Vapes Onboard

An Alaska Airlines flight from Seattle to Sacramento had to request police to meet the aircraft at the gate after a passenger allegedly vaped.

In-flight vaping incidents are escalating to law enforcement interventions, signaling growing crew safety concerns and potential regulatory pressure on airlines to implement stricter enforcement measures.

See Simple Flying →

PAI
Regulatory Mar 29, 2026

SMS Declaration of Compliance

What the SMS Declaration of Compliance means for operators and what the FAA expects.

SMS Declaration of Compliance demonstrates FAA's shift from prescriptive oversight to accountability-based safety management, requiring operators to self-certify systematic hazard identification rather than merely comply with checkboxes.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Safety Mar 29, 2026

I’m A Flight Attendant: This Is Why Boarding Is The Most Stressful Part Of The Job

A lot of responsibility in a short time.

Boarding's compressed timeline forces flight attendants to simultaneously manage safety protocols, passenger flow, and cabin setup, creating cognitive overload that compounds fatigue across multiple daily flights.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 29, 2026

6 Military Aircraft With The Most Complex Avionics

The flying supercomputers of 2026.

Modern military aircraft avionics now integrate AI-assisted systems and sensor fusion capabilities that require specialized maintenance protocols commercial airlines are beginning to adopt.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Why Do Airlines Keep Older Aircraft In Service Longer Than Expected?

Older aircraft come with higher operating costs, but have unique capabilities and have already been paid off by their operator.

Paid-off aircraft generate higher margins despite elevated maintenance costs, making fleet retention economically rational when replacement capital costs and financing burdens exceed operational expenses.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Mar 29, 2026

Surface Safety Spotlight – Los Angeles International Airport, CA (LAX) – From the Flight Deck

Los Angeles International Airport (LAX) is one of the nation’s busiest commercial airports. This short video highlights a runway incursion hazard where pilots exiting Runway 24R, particularly via...

Runway exit design at major hubs like LAX creates predictable incursion patterns that pilots should anticipate during high-traffic operations to prevent costly incidents.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

JetBlue Passenger With Highest Mosaic Status Caught Performing Inappropriate Act Inflight

The ACARS message sent during JetBlue Flight 2858 revealed a shocking act taking place in the cabin, involving a passenger in Mint.

High-status frequent flyer misbehavior exposes gaps in cabin crew monitoring systems, potentially prompting airlines to recalibrate enforcement standards regardless of passenger tier.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

After 20 Years, The Airbus A380 Still Shapes Airport Design

A closer look at some of the upgrades that airports had to make in order to accommodate the superjumbo.

A380 infrastructure investments remain sunk costs for airports that must justify underutilized gates and taxiways as airlines retire the superjumbo fleet.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 28, 2026

Northrop Grumman Testbed With AIM-260-Like Nose Spotted Flying Alongside Raytheon’s VOODOO 1 at Eglin AFB

The two testbeds, known by their callsigns SCAN 06 and VOODOO 1, carried out concurrent operations in Eglin’s highly instrumented airspace. For three consecutive days, on Mar. 24, 25 and 26, two testbed aircraft, the Northrop Grumman CRJ-700 carrying the U.S. registration code N806X and typically...

Concurrent testing of advanced air-to-air missile systems by competing defense contractors suggests accelerated development timelines for next-generation air combat capabilities.

See The Aviationist →

▶
General Aviation Mar 29, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Eastern Iowa Airport, IA (CID)

Eastern Iowa Airport (CID) is a medium-sized multi-use airport located south of Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Traffic includes basic training aircraft up through air carrier aircraft. Flight schools often use...

Eastern Iowa Airport's diverse traffic mix from training aircraft to air carriers positions it as a critical regional hub balancing general aviation development with commercial air service.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Aerospace Mar 28, 2026

Starfish Space finds a new partner for docking demonstration mission

Starfish Space has changed plans for a docking demonstration mission after its original partner backed out.

Starfish Space's ability to secure alternative partnerships for critical orbital servicing demonstrations signals growing industry confidence in commercial space infrastructure.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 28, 2026

Germany Considering MQ-28A Ghost Bat CCA, Says Defense Minister

German Defense Minister Boris Pistorius said the MQ-28 Ghost Bat is among the uncrewed aircraft being evaluated to support the Eurofighter Typhoon fighter fleet. Germany’s defense minister Boris Pistorius said during his visit to Australia on Mar. 27, 2026, that Berlin is considering Boeing Defence...

Germany's evaluation of the MQ-28 Ghost Bat represents NATO's expanding commitment to autonomous collaborative combat aircraft that extend fighter fleet capabilities beyond traditional crewed operations.

See The Aviationist →

Military Mar 28, 2026

USMC F-35Cs and USS Tripoli ARG Arrive in the Middle East

A month after the start of the hostilities, the U.S. Marine Corps is increasing its presence in the Middle East with the arrival of a F-35C squadron and the USS Tripoli Amphibious Ready Group. Mar. 28, 2026, marks a month since the U.S. and Israel launched Operations Epic Fury and Roaring Lion,...

The U.S. Marine Corps deployed F-35C carrier-based fighters alongside an amphibious ready group to escalate regional power projection capabilities during active Middle East hostilities.

See The Aviationist →

Industry Mar 29, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across all aviation layers—airline, airport, and flight operations—offers immediate capacity and efficiency gains without waiting for new aircraft technology maturation.

See MDPI →

Drones Mar 28, 2026

Robinson Unmanned

Robinson Unmanned (formerly Ascent AeroSystems) is the uncrewed aircraft systems (UAS) business unit of Robinson...

Robinson's transition to dedicated UAS operations signals traditional rotorcraft manufacturers pivoting toward autonomous systems to compete in the growing uncrewed market.

Drones Mar 28, 2026

Quantum Systems Strengthens Ukraine’s Air Defence Capabilities with 15,000 Interceptor Contract and WIY Drones Investment

In alignment with the defence priorities set out by the Ukrainian Government, Quantum Systems has...

Quantum Systems' investment in Ukrainian drone manufacturing signals a strategic shift toward domestic production capacity for allied defence systems amid prolonged conflict.

See sUAS News →

International Mar 28, 2026

Singapore delays SAF levy rollout amid Middle East conflict

As the war in Iran continues to roil world oil markets, Singapore has delayed the rollout of its…...

Geopolitical volatility and fuel price uncertainty are forcing governments to recalibrate aviation tax implementation timelines, signaling broader operational cost pressures ahead.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 28, 2026

Artemis 2 astronauts arrive at KSC

The four Artemis 2 astronauts arrived in Florida March 27 for final preparations ahead of a launch still scheduled as soon as April 1.

The Artemis 2 crew's arrival marks the final operational phase of NASA's lunar return mission, with launch readiness checks now underway at Kennedy Space Center.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 27, 2026

[Updated] Iranian Attack on Prince Sultan Air Base Reportedly Struck U.S. KC-135s, E-3 Sentry

Satellite images show signs of an attack at Prince Sultan Air Base, Saudi Arabia, with The Wall Street Journal now reporting that an Iranian missile damaged “several U.S. refueling aircraft.” Satellite images emerged on Mar. 27, 2026, appeared to show signs of damage on an apron used by U.S....

Damage to KC-135 tankers directly impacts U.S. air operations' refueling capacity and mission range across the Middle East theater.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

I Am Artemis: Erik Richards

Listen to this audio excerpt from Erik Richards, Near Space Network Mission Manager: For Erik Richards, supporting NASA’s first crewed Artemis mission to the Moon and back is the culmination of a career spent helping spacecraft communicate with Earth. Like many kids who grew up at the height of the...

Mission control infrastructure built over decades is proving critical to Artemis success, as seasoned engineers like Richards leverage existing deep-space communication networks to enable lunar return operations.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Selects Intuitive Machines to Deliver Artemis Science, Tech to Moon

NASA has awarded Intuitive Machines of Houston, $180.4 million to deliver NASA-funded science and technology to the lunar surface as part of the agency’s CLPS (Commercial Lunar Payload Services) initiative and Artemis program. This lunar delivery, which includes seven payloads — five of them NASA’s...

NASA's $180.4M contract with Intuitive Machines signals accelerating commercialization of lunar logistics, reducing government's direct infrastructure costs while building supply chains for sustained Artemis operations.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA’s Environment and Energy “Blue Marble” Awards Categories

Category I: NASA Environmental Quality Award Recognizes excellence in environmental management and planning, including stewardship of natural and cultural resources. This category highlights achievements in compliance, conservation, remediation, communication, and environmental information...

NASA's environmental awards framework incentivizes aerospace contractors and facilities to integrate sustainability practices into operations, creating compliance benchmarks across the industry supply chain.

See NASA →

Regulatory Mar 27, 2026

EASA warns non-airworthy stolen engine parts could re-enter the market

The European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) is warning airlines, maintenance providers and parts buyers to be on…...

Stolen aircraft engine parts from a security breach pose immediate operational risks, requiring enhanced verification protocols across the supply chain to prevent catastrophic failures.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

Commercial Space Federation (CSF) Welcomes Two New Associate Members

March 27, 2026 – Washington, D.C.—The Commercial Space Federation (CSF) is pleased to welcome Astrolab and Zeno Power, two innovative companies advancing planetary mobility and reliable energy in extreme environments. […]...

CSF's addition of Astrolab and Zeno Power signals commercial space sector consolidation around lunar surface infrastructure, addressing the critical mobility and power challenges that will enable sustained deep-space operations.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

ESA to decide by June on Europe’s Gateway contributions

MUNICH — For more than a year, questions have swirled about Artemis changes and the Gateway’s role in U.S. lunar ambitions. Following NASA’s March 24 decision to halt work on Gateway, the lunar-orbiting station that had been intended to support astronauts before and after lunar surface missions,...

Europe's delayed Gateway decision reflects NASA's strategic pivot away from lunar-orbit infrastructure, forcing ESA to reassess its Artemis commitments amid shifting U.S. program priorities.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 27, 2026

Gallery: Luke Days 2026

A glimpse back at last weekend's air show at Luke Air Force Base in Arizona.

Luke Air Force Base air shows demonstrate ongoing public engagement strategies that maintain community support for military aviation operations and pilot training programs.

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR’s View of Mount Rainier

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount Rainier. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

SAR satellite technology's all-weather imaging capability enables precise volcanic and geological monitoring critical for infrastructure planning in populated regions.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR Views Mount St. Helens

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount St. Helens. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

NISAR's L-band synthetic aperture radar capability enables all-weather monitoring of volcanic and geological features critical for disaster preparedness and earth observation applications.

See NASA →
